---
internal: true
origin: https://github.com/JerryI/wljs-editor/tree/main
---
import Notebook from '@site/src/components/wljs-notebook-react';

Used __to decorate wrapped__ Wolfram Expression with custom HTML elements

```mathematica
(*BB[*)(expr)(*,*)(*view*)(*]BB*)
```

Most decorations generated by `Frame` and etc...

- the expression is __editable__
- a common WL editor is spawned inside
- only the container of the editor can be decorated

:::tip
See the full guide on them [Decorations](../../../Development/Decorations.md)
:::

There is a helper function to prevent an editor from rendering, if you want to write using this low-level representation

```mathematica
BoxBox[expr_Boxes | _String, decorator_]
```

it will substitute `expr` into a subeditor and decorate it using `decorator` expression executed on WLJS Interpreter.

## Examples
### Change the display value and call arbitrary WLJS function
This is a dummy example 
```mathematica
special /: MakeBoxes[special, StandardForm] := BoxBox["Decoration", Alert["Hi"]]
special
```

### Styling expression using custom JS
One can define its own style of cells boxes

```js
.js
core.Styler = async (args, env) => {
  env.element.style.boxShadow = "1px 1px 5px black";
}
```

```mathematica
wrapper /: MakeBoxes[wrapper[expr_], StandardForm] := BoxBox[RowBox[{"wrapper[", MakeBoxes[expr, StandardForm], "]"}], Styler]
```

and then try

```mathematica
wrapper[1/2]
```

__Result__

<Notebook code="" name="cabinetmaking-0d307">cabinetmaking-0d307</Notebook>

__The neat thing__ is if you edit an expression, it will also change the code underneath.
